self_management.definition: Umbrella term for self-care, coping, and any forms of managing own MH outside of formal care

self_management_reason.definition: Student describes why they chose to manage their own mental health, or why they chose a specific self-management strategy
examples: self-managing because of previous negative experience with treatment
self-managing because of a dispositional preference to be independent
supplements treatment with self-management because of limited therapy sessions

self_management_description.definition: Student describes the type of self-management they engage in; any practice or tool
examples: going on walk to try and manage mental health did not work
being with fellow church congregants as a source of support
attending an online counselling course on building resilience
maintaining boundaries in challenging relationships

self_management_challenges.definition: Barriers to self-management; failed attempts at self-management. From the student's POV
examples: schoolwork or other responsibilities take up too much time
student tried journalling, but it does not help manage their mental health
student feels like they burden friends by reaching out so they do not access social support

self_management_harmful.definition: Student describes engaging in harmful practices to try and manage their mental health
examples: physical self-harm to try and achieve mental health relief
substance use to try and detach from problems
endlessly scrolling on phone as a form of disengagement
excessive exercise

seeking_formal_care.definition: A student describes any experiences SEEKING formal mental health care (e.g., therapist, counselor, psychiatrist/doctor, psychologist). Alternatively, in sites/situations where formal services may not be an option, an entity/individual whom the student considers to have expertise

seeking_care_reason_experience.definition: Students described what made them seek formal services and why; the tipping point
examples: seeks services because frightened from suicidal thoughts
seeks services because told by partner
seeks services because no longer able to study
seeks services because of weight loss that turns out to be mental health-related
voluntary or involuntary hospitalization

seeking_care_reason_intention.definition: Student discusses what hypothetically would or will make them seek care in the future
examples: if I experience grief in my life, I will probably seek therapy
I would like to enter therapy for xyz at some point, I haven't yet, but it's on my to do list
my psychiatrist told me to reach out and set up an appointment with her if I xyz

seeking_care_description.definition: A student describes how they reached out to care providers
examples: browse therapy clinics and contact the one with therapist headshots
download app and schedule first appointment with digital provider
ask friend for support to contact services together
Re-contact the therapist from before

seeking_care_challenges.definition: Barriers to formal care; failed attempts at seeking care. External setback the student encountered once they had actually taken a step to seek care

seeking_care_reluctance.definition: Describes any reluctance to seek professional care, including not wanting to seek care, believing that care will not be helpful or 'not for me', etc

care_encounter.definition: A student describes any experiences RECEIVING formal mental health care

care_encounter_description.definition: A student discusses their experiences of receiving and/or remaining in formal mental health services

care_encounter_unpleasant.definition: A student discusses any negative, unpleasant or harmful experiences they've had of receiving formal mental health services

care_encounter_ending.definition: Discusses reasons for or experiences with ending (or extensively pausing) care at any stage of formal care

mental_health_beliefs.definition: A student describes mis/understandings or 'meta' commentary about mental health/illness, care, or treatment

problem_recognition.definition: Interviewee describes the process of recognition or identifying that a mental health-related issue is a 'problem'
examples: learning in class about the symptoms of depression and realizing that it is what they experience
realizing that anxiety is normalized in their community
first time experiencing a mental illness episode
having a 'wake-up call' experience

understanding_mental_illness.definition: Describes participant's level of knowledge, awareness, and understanding of mental health and illness
examples: I think depression is mostly genetic
Anxiety is caused by too much social media
ADHD is being diagnosed more often these days
Depression is just feeling sad a lot

recovery_and_treatment.definition: Describes participant's beliefs about what recovery or mental well-being means
examples: You can learn to cope, but mental illness never really goes away
I used to think recovery meant being happy all the time
I believe healing comes from within, not from external treatments
Therapy is helpful if you find the right therapist

self_identity.definition: A student discusses demographic or social/political/cultural identities

other_individual_factors.definition: Any other internal or person-specific factors that impact mental health and help-seeking not included in the codebook

daily_functioning.definition: A student describes their ability to function and participate in daily activities and fulfill life responsibilities

physical_health_symptoms.definition: A student describes how physical health impacts their wellbeing or help-seeking

diet_exercise.definition: A student discusses anything related to food/nutrition/dietary/exercise behaviors

sleep.definition: A student discusses anything related to sleep

substance_use.definition: A student describes thoughts or experiences related to using substances

stress.definition: A participant describes experiences of stress, pressure, or tension

stressor.definition: A participant specifies an external circumstance that is causing them stress

stress_response.definition: A participant describes experiences of stress/pressure/tension AND a physiological/physical, (social) behavioral, emotional, or cognitive response

trauma.definition: Witnessing, experiencing, or being threatened with death, serious injury, or physical or sexual violence

guilt_shame.definition: A student describes feelings of guilt or shame

psychological_pain.definition: Describes any internal experiences of severe emotional distress, overwhelming emotional pain, or overall aversiveness towards life

nssi.definition: Describes hurting themselves on purpose without the intent to die, for reasons that are not socially sanctioned

suicide_ideation.definition: Student has thoughts pertaining to suicide/ending own life

suicide_method.definition: Describes having had any direct or indirect method/plan to end life

suicide_behavior.definition: Describes any previous suicide-related behaviors enacted by the student

suicide_reason_dying.definition: Describes any external or internal events/factors that gave rise to thoughts or desires to die

suicide_reason_not_dying.definition: Why hasn't the student attempted? What has stopped/prevented the student from dying by suicide?

suicide_other.definition: Witnessing, observing, or hearing about someone else's suicidal intent, ideation, method/plan, attempt or deaths

social_relationships_family.definition: Discussion of family relationships impact on mental health or help-seeking

social_relationships_friends.definition: Discussion of friend/roommate/peer relationships impact on mental health or help-seeking

social_relationships_romantic.definition: Discussion of romantic/sexual relationships impact on mental health or help-seeking

social_relationships_pet.definition: Discussion of pet relationships impact on mental health or help-seeking

social_relationships_faculty.definition: Discussion of faculty/supervisor relationships impact on mental health or help-seeking

social_relationships_other.definition: Discussion of other relationship types impact on mental health or help-seeking

social_relationships_bullying.definition: Bullying occurring at the peer level, usually in school environment

social_relationships_negative.definition: Any descriptions of social difficulties/discomforts/conflicts (other than bullying)

university_life.definition: A student describes aspects of life as a university student

university_academics.definition: Describes how grades/academic performance impact mental health and help-seeking behaviors

university_transitions.definition: Major life transitions that happened around/during college

university_dropout.definition: A student mentions thoughts, intentions or experiences related to dropping out from college

university_services.definition: A student describes mental health services at the university

physical_environment.definition: Describes physical spaces/facilities, infrastructure, green spaces, living conditions/arrangements, and other aspects of the physical environment

healthcare_system.definition: A student describes experiences of systemic or logistical aspects of the healthcare system

finances.definition: Any discussion of finances that may impact wellbeing or help-seeking

stigma.definition: A student describes stigma towards mental health / mental health care that they have perceived, experienced, held, or otherwise observed

social_climate.definition: Societal events, geopolitical conflicts/climate, government policies and legislation, or systemic inequalities and/or discrimination

covid.definition: Anything related to COVID, such as the societal impact and academic impact

media.definition: A student discusses the role of media/technology in mental health

religion_spirituality.definition: A student discusses spirituality/religion of their own or that of parents, peers/community
examples: reading biblical texts, praying, attending church
community activities and traditions based on religion
ethics/morals/worldviews that are based on understanding of theology
